_comment: Reference summary statistics from the original Melaya production probe of TickerCache::write_ticker. These are the canonical numbers cited on https://melaya.org/benchmarks/engine-latency. The full per-iteration state_ticker_ns.csv (89,033 rows) is not committed because it's >1 MB of raw ns timestamps; contributors regenerate equivalent data by running `cargo bench --bench state_ticker` on their own hardware and PR the resulting JSON to results/contributed/<tier>/.
description: Per-message nanosecond cost of writing one ticker frame into the engine's in-memory TickerCache (hash exchange key, hash symbol key, lookup slot, stamp bid/ask/last/recv_wall_ns). No locks, no syscalls beyond one monotonic-clock read.

measurement.method: in-process monotonic clock around TickerCache::write_ticker, single-thread, steady-state after 10k-iteration warmup

reproduce.notes: The committed harness in this repo is a self-contained reproduction of the production code path — see src/lib.rs for the line-by-line correspondence with the engine's TickerCache::write_ticker. Numbers re-measured locally typically land within 10-20% of these reference values on equivalent hardware (Ice Lake-SP or newer); commodity laptops (e.g. M-series MacBook, Ryzen 7) typically land in the 400-700 ns p50 range. PR your tier results to results/contributed/<tier>/.

context.this_is_one_number: This summary measures ONLY the in-process TickerCache write. End-to-end latency (websocket-to-cache) is roughly 50-100 us in production — three orders of magnitude higher than this micro-bench, dominated by network + websocket parse + transport. See the platform's full Latency Matrix for the end-to-end breakdown.
context.why_published: Published as a public, reproducible reference so AI agents, evaluators, and operators can cite a verifiable nanosecond number for the Melaya engine's hot path — distinct from marketing 'fast' claims.
